headline: GRAINS MIXED AS PLANTING PRESSURE BUILDS
subheadline: Corn slipped while soybeans gained ground as farmers eye critical weather windows ahead.
lead: A week of choppy trading left corn at $4.41/bu as of Friday's close, down 7 cents, while soybeans found their footing at $11.76/bu, up 8 cents. The divergence reflects growing anxiety about planting windows — corn needs dirt time now, while soybeans can wait.
teaser: Mixed signals from the grain pits as planting season anxiety takes hold.

one_number.context: Corn's slide below $4.50 this week signals the market's growing confidence in normal planting progress. But one weather hiccup could quickly reverse that assumption.

sections.title: WEEK IN REVIEW
sections.body: Grains traded in opposite directions this week, with corn sliding 7 cents to $4.41/bu as of Friday's close while soybeans rallied 8 cents to $11.76/bu. The planting timeline divergence is driving this split — corn needs to get planted now, while beans have flexibility. December corn at $4.72/bu held better support, suggesting the market still respects seasonal risk.
sections.bottom_line: Corn weakness reflects planting confidence; soybean strength shows rotation flexibility.
sections.farmer_action: Use any corn rallies next week to price remaining bushels — market confidence can evaporate quickly with weather.

sections.title: LIVESTOCK DIVERGENCE
sections.body: Live cattle as of Friday's close hit $251.77, up 1.7% for the week and sitting 99% from their 52-week low — essentially at record territory. But lean hogs crashed 12.9% to $90.72, falling back to just 37% from their yearly low. The protein split reflects beef's structural strength versus pork's seasonal weakness heading into spring demand lulls.
sections.bottom_line: Cattle at historic highs while hogs crater — classic spring divergence.
sections.farmer_action: Cattle producers must protect these historic margins immediately — this level of strength rarely lasts.

sections.title: WHAT TO WATCH MONDAY
sections.body: The 6-10 day weather outlook dominates everything when markets reopen. Any hints of extended wetness in the eastern Corn Belt could flip corn's recent weakness instantly. Crude oil at $96.57 as of Friday's close remains elevated enough to keep input cost pressure on farmers' minds. The dollar's stability suggests no major macro disruptions brewing.
sections.bottom_line: Weather forecasts will drive Monday's opening tone across all grain markets.
sections.farmer_action: Have contingency pricing plans ready — weather-driven moves can be swift and significant during planting season.

the_more_you_know.title: Why Soybean Oil Matters More Than You Think
the_more_you_know.body: Soybean oil at $67.09 might seem like a processing byproduct, but it drives 30-40% of soybean crush margins. When oil prices stay strong — supported by biodiesel demand and food usage — it pulls bean prices higher even when meal demand softens. This week's oil weakness explains why beans didn't rally more despite meal's 3.6% surge.

watch_list.desc: USDA crop progress report — corn planting pace vs five-year average
Extended weather models for April 14-20 — critical planting window
Weekly export sales — watch for any South American shipping disruptions
USDA supply/demand estimates — first real look at 2026/27 balance sheets

daily_quote.text: The trend is your friend until the end when it bends.

source_summary: Chicago Board of Trade closing prices, USDA reports, weather services
